title: TBPN March 26 2026 — ARM Chips, Meta/YouTube Lawsuit, Salt & Stone Exit, Tesla Algorithm, Linear AI, Suno 300M ARR
guest: Eric Goldman, Neema Jalali, Jon McNeill, Karri Saarinen, Mikey Shulman, Ida Bardari, Demi Clari, Zack Kanter, Nick (This Week in Fintech), Zach Perret

chapters.title: Welcome to the Show: Today's Guests and Opening Remarks
chapters.text:
You're watching TV. Today is Thursday, March 26th. We are live from the TBP and Ultra on the temple of technology, the Four.

Speaker 2: Finance the capital of capital.

Speaker 1: Let me also pull up the linear line up because we have a monster show. Geordie's back in the TBP and Ultra Dome. I know you missed him. We have Eric Goldman coming on to help breakdown the meta YouTube lawsuit.

Speaker 2: By the way, yesterday was the first time we've been live that I was not on the air and so I was watching on the 3rd.

Speaker 1: How did I do? Give me a review, how did I do?

Speaker 2: I thought you did fantastic. I was texting you in real time. I probably sent you like 20 something messages.

Speaker 1: Somebody said I was free soloing TVBN and it was great. But we really bored down to our last few associates here. Tyler was out, Geordie was out, Nick was out. We were on a shoestring.

Speaker 2: We have Nima friend of mine, founder of Salt and Stone. They recently exited the business, a massive multi 100 million dollar deal. We have John McNeil coming on to talk about Elon Musk's five step algorithm for scaling Tesla. Then we have Kari from Linear on how linear is evolving in the AI age. And then we have a bunch of different founders, including Mikey from Suno.

Speaker 1: Then we got back-to-back Zacks. Zack Cantor, Zack Pere, back-to-back Zacks.

chapters.title: ARM Enters Chip Market: $15B Bet and Industry Dynamics
chapters.text:
Speaker 1: The big news of the week is that ARM, the intellectual property developer that creates IP designs for CPUs, is now getting into the chip game. The company is up 15% over the last few days on the news that they will sell their own chips. This is new for ARM, a very old company with fascinating history. They normally just license out their intellectual property and that is a phenomenal business. 97% gross margins.

It's a big business, 4 billion in revenue last year, nearly 800 million of net income. They're expecting to ramp revenue to $15 billion by 2031. The market cap for ARM is now around $166 billion.

Speaker 2: 4 billion last year, currently trading at 165 billion.

Speaker 1: This is a big shift in strategy. ARM hasn't gotten the attention that other GPU makers like NVIDIA have received. CPUs are far from dead though. We are in what seems like a CPU crunch. Intel can't make CPUs fast enough. NVIDIA is starting to sell their Grace CPU that goes with Hopper. Agents need CPUs. You need to fill the GPUs constantly with new data and tasks. All of the agents use CPUs to make web queries, search the web, run Python, spin up web servers.

We talked to folks at Semi Analysis about this — uptime is going down for non-GPU accelerated workloads. You go to a SaaS product running on a CPU somewhere in a data center and it isn't loading today. We need more CPUs as well as GPUs.

ARM started basically as a joint venture between Apple, Acorn Computer and VLSI. They needed to design a low power CPU for mobile devices before phones — PDAs. ARM sets out to be the global standard for CPUs and they create the ISA, the instruction set architecture. Apple pays a license to ARM for every chip Apple sells.

Speaker 2: SoftBank owns roughly 90% of the company. They bought the entire company for around 25-30 billion USD in 2016. Their holdings in ARM are somewhere in the range of 140 billion. They've massively levered up against that position.

Speaker 1: ARM licenses to a ton of different tech companies. Amazon uses ARM for the Graviton chips. A lot of Android phone makers have ARM based chips. But now ARM is going to be making the chips themselves and they're going to be working with Meta Platforms and OpenAI. This means a shift in the economics. 97% gross margins for licensing will be closer to 50%, but offset by huge gains in market size and revenue. The company trades at roughly 90 times forward earnings.

Ben Thompson pointed out NVIDIA sells an ARM-based Grace CPU, so they're competing but also working together challenging X86 options from Intel and AMD. Meta announced a new partnership with ARM to collaborate on purpose-built CPUs for AI infrastructure. ARM called it the ARM AGI CPU.

chapters.title: Sanders & AOC Propose Bill to Halt Data Center Construction
chapters.text:
Speaker 1: Let's talk about this data centre moratorium bill. Senator Bernie Sanders and AOC introduced the AI Data Centre Moratorium Act of 2026, that if enacted would require all current and planned data centres to halt construction. It would even block upgrading existing data centers. They define data centers based on power demands, cooling capabilities.

I was thinking like I don't need any more AI data centers at this point. I just want to build AGI data centers and ASI data centers.

Speaker 2: The bill would halt all new data center construction and upgrades until more legislation guarantees: AI is safe and effective, economic gains benefit workers not just wealthy owners of big tech, and AI does not increase electricity or utility prices, harm communities or destroy the environment.

Speaker 1: It depends on how you define safe and effective. The FDA model would really slow things down. They release drugs with side effects all the time. You take this, it cures your cancer, but it's going to make you throw up. People say yeah, I'll take that trade.

Speaker 2: They are using AI leaders' own statements against them. Elon Musk said he had a lot of AI nightmares and would certainly slow down AI if he could. Demis Hassabis said he would support an AI pause if other countries also paused. Dario Amodei said he was absolutely in favor of trying to slow down if other countries also slowed down. But there is zero movement on getting other countries to slow down.

Speaker 3: Xi Jinping chaired a rare Politburo study session on AI warning of unprecedented risks. AI safety is listed alongside pandemics and cyber attacks in their national emergency response plan.

Speaker 1: The ACT has a long way to go. As written, the requirements in the bill would be an incredible gift to America's adversaries and catastrophic for overall AI progress. But it could be popular though. There are already candidates who have made pausing data centre build outs their main thing.

Speaker 2: The space data center people are saying space data centers don't seem so silly now. Data centers don't create a lot of jobs locally but they generate massive amounts of local tax revenue.

chapters.title: Jury Finds Meta & YouTube Liable in Addiction Trial
chapters.text:
Speaker 1: The Wall Street Journal front page says Meta YouTube found addictive, harmful. California jurors say the tech companies designed their apps to cause injury to kids. Very bad. The total damages are roughly 6 million total, 3 million each company.

This has much broader implications because this is precedent setting and there will be a flood of other cases. Both Meta and YouTube liable for a 20 year old woman's mental health crisis in a bellwether trial that treated platforms as defective products and potentially marks the end to the absolute immunity nature of Section 230.

The plaintiff's lawyer Mark Lanier argued that Meta and YouTube built digital casinos that use neurobiological techniques similar to those employed by slot machines.

The jury found specific features are designed to be addictive: Infinite scroll creates an environment where there are no natural stopping points. Algorithmic recommendation feeds users highly engaging content. Autoplay removes users' agency. Notifications exploit their need for validation. Instagram beauty filters contributed to body dysmorphia. Features like the Like button exploit users' biological need for societal approval.

Taylor Lorenz had some great takes. She asked if Spotify addicted you to music by playing good songs on demand.

This is obviously a trivial amount of money for these companies, but the bellwether nature has significant implications. There are over 10,000 individual personal injury cases, almost 800 school district claims, and 40 state level cases pending nationwide.

The case is not attacking Section 230 because it's not about user-generated content. They went after the like button, the infinite scroll, the recommendation feeds — the features built by the platforms themselves.

Speaker 2: Meta trading down almost 9% today. There's new legal risk, thousands of lawsuits floating out there. The real question is do they have to make product level changes that impact time spent in the app and the advertising business.

chapters.title: High-Speed Trains & The Ethics of Shoplifting
chapters.text:
Speaker 1: In China, high speed trains have become a sightseeing attraction. I would definitely go see the high speed train rip through the countryside.

Speaker 2: Also, this is the most incredible video of China I've ever seen. Beautiful mountain range and sunset.

Speaker 1: Where do you stand on shoplifting? I'm anti. The Wall Street Journal wrote a piece by Emma Camp. There's a new generation of shoplifters who are fairly affluent and are sneaking out luxury goods.

According to a 2025 survey, the likelihood that someone would purposefully take an item at self checkout without scanning actually increased with income. The rich are stealing from the rich.

A viral article in Curbed profiled shoplifters caught stealing from Whole Foods. None were acting out of necessity. Most stole luxury goods — $30 eye cream, strip steak, fancy organic chocolate. None expressed guilt.

Emma Camp writes: Petty theft is a vice for a certain kind of loser. Many are creative types — photographers, graphic designers, sculptors, musicians. Presumably highly educated but downwardly mobile. They steal because they feel entitled to the kind of life where they can thoughtlessly drop $50 on French cheese and sushi rolls while paying Manhattan rent.

This is a teenager's leftism, one in which fighting the man means ripping him off. It's a post hoc justification for a base impulse, the belief that everything you want should be free.

Businesses hike prices to make up for shoplifting losses. Public transportation crumbles without sufficient revenue. As journalist Kelsey Piper put it: when you shoplift, you directly and unambiguously impoverish your community.

chapters.title: Public Messaging on AI, Sora Video Generation & Industry Metrics
chapters.text:
Speaker 1: Noah Smith wrote AI has the worst sales pitch I've ever seen. Quote: Our product will make you economically useless and possibly kill you is not a value proposition. AI leaders need to change their public messaging fast.

His satire: Hi, I'm from the cursed microwave company. Our product perfectly cooks your food but also microwaves your whole body so you're paralyzed and unable to work. Oh by the way, there's a 2 to 25% chance our microwaves destroy the entire human race.

I completely agree. It's very important that AI lab leaders focus on the actual tangible benefits. People are afraid of AI but a billion people are using ChatGPT to get answers. This disconnect cannot last.

Speaker 2: The phrase tech lash was originally coined by Adrian Wooldridge in the Economist in 2013. The second tech clash has begun because the average American believes AI is now a threat to their way of life, starting at the bottom of Maslow's hierarchy.

Speaker 1: The Sora news is interesting. Sora jumped ahead of Meta Vibes very quickly. The cost of a video model feels immense. There were rumors of spending 10-15 million a day on compute just for Sora.

Speaker 2: Mitchell Green's hierarchy of BS from cold calling 10,000 companies: If companies have good cash profits, they report those. If not, adjusted profits. Then gross profits, revenue, adjusted revenue like GMV, monthly active users, subscribers, downloads, page views, and finally that they were voted best place to work.

Speaker 1: Lines of code per year run rate is an insane stat.

Gwen Shotwell is on the cover of Time magazine. SpaceX is racing to build the most powerful rockets yet.

Speaker 2: FT reports SpaceX IPO bankers toying with allowing existing shareholders to sell on day one. Musk's influence over the listing has been highly unusual. xAI co-founder Manuel Kroyce is departing — the last co-founder outside of Elon.

Meek Mill says he's getting $250 per tweet. He's gone on a big tear promoting AI, specifically Claude.

chapters.title: Eric Goldman on Meta & YouTube Addiction Trial Implications
chapters.text:
Eric Goldman: I'm a professor of Law and associate dean for research at Santa Clara University School of Law. I've been doing Internet law for over 30 years.

In California State court in Los Angeles, hundreds of plaintiffs filed lawsuits against social media services. These are joined together but not a class action. A similar case is in federal court in Northern California, a multi district litigation with thousands of plaintiffs including school districts and Native American tribes.

They picked 3 plaintiffs for bellwether trials. The first jury awarded $6 million. Far less than plaintiffs requested, far more than defendants hoped. If you multiply by 3000 potential plaintiffs, that translates to about $20 billion. Meta and Google maybe have that, but TikTok and Snap maybe don't.

The appeals will go as high as courts will take them. The difficulty with settlement is these are not class actions, so defendants cannot settle against all potential plaintiffs.

I'm not a fan of the tobacco analogy. Tobacco has no health benefits. Social media has many social benefits — thriving communities. Social media is a mix of potentially helpful and harmful things. In the end, what people are being addicted to is people talking to each other.

The bigger question isn't will we lose the like button. The more serious outcome is what social media services drop out of the industry entirely. I don't think we should assume social media will exist in its current form.

States have already passed laws banning infinite scrolling, autoplay, the like button. Warning labels are subject to constitutional challenges and probably won't be the final resolution.

chapters.title: From Pro Snowboarder to Building Salt & Stone: A Profitable Journey
chapters.text:
Speaker 6: My name is Neema Jalali. I'm the founder and CEO of Salt and Stone. I was a pro snowboarder for about 10 years, from about 20 to 30. More freestyle, in movies and magazines.

Speaker 2: There's basically 2 paths in pro snowboarding — the lifestyle guy making movies, and the competition track going to Olympics and X Games.

Speaker 6: You pick a song for your part. Everyone wants to get last part in the video, that's most prestigious. It's not a team sport. There are a lot of lessons — like learning to kickflip takes 6 months of just failing.

I knew I wanted to do Salt and Stone. I wanted to see how far I could go if I just went all in 100%. The first product was sunscreen. The brand was profitable from day one. We had about 40-50 individual retailers lined up.

Speaker 2: How quickly did you realize the product was special? I've seen exceptional teams that just don't go anywhere because the product doesn't sell itself. When I first tried your deodorant after trying 20 different better-for-you deodorants, I was like, wow, this is incredible.

Speaker 6: We did something in deodorant that hadn't been done before in clean deodorant. Why shouldn't it smell as good as your perfume or cologne? I was really making it for myself. It turns out what I wanted was what everybody else wanted.

Speaker 2: Did the company ever almost die?

Speaker 6: No. It's been a dream come true.

Speaker 2: You one-shotted entrepreneurship. $165 million last year.

Speaker 6: First three years was just myself. Then I hired somebody for ops. We were just two people for about 3 years after that. The team is now 50-55, all remote. No sales reps ever. You've got to create a brand that the retailer wants.

I chose Advent as the partner because of their international expertise. My goal was never to just exit fully. I want my grandkids to look at this thing and be like, that's what my grandfather built.

My advice: go all in. Forget work life balance. You've got to be competitive and go after the big guys. Just go for it.

chapters.title: Jon McNeill on Elon Musk's 5-Step Algorithm for Scaling Tesla
chapters.text:
Jon McNeill: I wanted to tell the story of how Tesla and SpaceX work on the inside. Walter Isaacson told me to write the business book. He said I don't write business books, I write character books.

The algorithm came from all the mistakes we made. Postmortems asking how did we get in this situation. The framework was designed to push decision making to the edge.

Step 1: Question every requirement. Is it a requirement of physics, safety, or law? It took 64 clicks to buy a Tesla online. 44 of those were in the auto loan documents. I asked the lawyers how many paragraphs were required by law. They said none. We got down to basically 4 sentences.

Step 2: Make it as simple as possible. Fewest steps, delete everything the customer doesn't pay for.

Step 3: Speed it up. Speed reveals all kinds of warts. Good, fast and cheap is actually achievable.

Step 4: Automate last. DoorDash started with a PDF and a phone number. Amazon ran to a bookstore to buy the book you ordered. The Model 3 production line failed when automated first. The only way to save the company was build a tent outside the factory and build by hand.

Step 5: Return to step 1.

Even after being public, Tesla operated on one quarter's worth of cash. Elon starves the balance sheet so the company stays sharp. He hires orthogonally — nobody has industry experience. Software-first mentality — one chip for autonomous driving, not 18-36 chips.

On Chinese competition: China subsidizes 100 market entrants, lets evolutionary biology play out, consolidates capacity under the top 3-5 winners, then exports to dominate. BYD, Geely, Volvo is owned by Geely. Zeekr provides the new Waymos. Chinese cars are already in America.

America needs more manufacturing engineers. Tim Cook says the best in the US fit in the Stanford auditorium. In China, they'd fill three Stanford football stadiums.

chapters.title: Linear's Evolution: Rethinking Issue Tracking with AI Agents
chapters.text:
Karri Saarinen: This week at Linear we wanted to share our thinking on issue tracking and project management. I wrote this essay saying issue tracking is dead.

Historically it's been a ticketing system — engineer doesn't do anything without a ticket. It's like a line cook system where someone yells fries and steak. Legacy platforms encouraged the complexity.

Customers come to us with 40-state bug tracking processes and 30 custom fields. Our answer is: the point of moving to Linear is rethinking this. If the agent can fix the bug in 5 minutes but the bug reporting process takes a week, that's silly.

The problems now are: 1) Context — how does the agent know what to work on? Linear can be that context system. 2) Agency — the system should become self-driving. Bugs get reported, Linear agents start debugging automatically, make a fix, then tag a human to verify.

We launched the Linear agent. I use it to understand underlying needs from feature requests. If you can build anything fast, the problem is deciding what to build and why.

We're building our own coding agent as a wrapper around existing agents. 75-77% of Linear workspaces have cloud agents. The remaining 25% face environment problems, timing, or policy blocking.

Some companies have a friction form — engineers file it when they want new tools, escalates to leadership with an SLA to resolve in a week. Forces procurement, IT and security to do something.

chapters.title: Genetically Engineering Chickens to Produce Medicines in Eggs
chapters.text:
Demi Clari: I'm the co-founder and CEO of Neon Bio. We genetically engineer chickens to produce medicines inside their eggs. I'm an aerospace engineer, my co-founder is an evolutionary biologist.

Most new medicines today are complex proteins. The way they're manufactured is expensive, reliant on fragile global supply chains, and makes drugs inaccessible to most people.

Chickens happen to be the universe's best protein factory. They've been honed by 250 million years of evolution to efficiently turn simple inputs — grain and water — into complex proteins inside their eggs. We harness that system to produce drugs like Humira or Keytruda.

We just announced a large commercial deal to bring up to three drugs to market. We're starting with biosimilars — known molecules, no molecular risk.

With a few thousand birds we can produce the global supply of a drug like Humira. A small farm is below 25,000 chickens. A chicken can treat 10 to 100 people in a year. We scale by breeding chickens — it's pretty linear.

chapters.title: Suno's Growth: AI-Driven Music Creation and Creative Entertainment
chapters.text:
Mikey Shulman: We're a consumer entertainment company. Creative entertainment. We are giving people the best, most fulfilling creative experiences of their lives.

Even Claude Code is creative entertainment — it's really fun to build things.

We hit 2 million subscribers, $300 million ARR. I wake up six days of the week thinking this is great, we're the only ones in consumer entertainment. The 7th day I think we must be missing something that all these giant companies know.

Our new model lets you use your own voice to make music that sounds like you. And if you're not the best singer, it'll sound like a better version of you. You can also upload tracks to inspire the model.

Almost 1/4 of all songs made on platform are derivatives of other songs — covers basically. There's a social ecosystem around these things.

We're not focused on B2B right now. We want to delight regular people who love music.

On artists using Suno: Every artist is using it, every artist thinks it's amazing. Nobody wants to talk about it yet because they're scared of the reaction. It's the GLP-1 of music — everyone's on it and no one wants to talk about it.

The consumption curve is much more even than Spotify. People fall in love with their own music as much as other people's music. It's all deep cuts.

chapters.title: Valence: Protecting Privacy with Smart Ultrasonic Jammer Technology
chapters.text:
Ida Bardari: I graduated with a degree in physics from Harvard and now I'm building De Valence, a company protecting your freedom.

We're building on existing ultrasonic jammer technology but making it smart — it takes in information from the nearby environment and targets distortion signals toward detected smart devices. An AI algorithm makes the distortion signals hard to decode.

The problem: AI wearables, Meta Ray-Bans, lots of unwanted recording happening. Confidential information and private conversations can be used to train AI models.

It's on/off for full autonomy. Pre-orders mainly consumer facing at around $1000. The processing algorithms require a lot of power, driving the cost. You need to find nearby devices, block them, and ensure blocking isn't reconstructible in post processing.

Certain blocking like RF jamming is not allowed because it interferes with emergency services. But ultrasonic methods have legal options.

chapters.title: Stedi: Powering Healthcare Transactions with EDI Technology
chapters.text:
Zack Kanter: I'm the founder and CEO of Stedi. We're a healthcare clearing house — the Stripe of healthcare. Healthcare is 18% of US GDP. We process tens of millions of real-time eligibility checks per month plus claims, adjudications, and payments.

We've seen a surprising number of small doctors and dentists offices signing up. Doctors are vibe coding revenue cycle management systems.

I previously started a brand of auto parts in Taiwan and China. I found out that retail logistics, supply chain, and manufacturing is powered by X12 EDI. I got so frustrated with EDI systems I built my own.

I thought it would cost 10 times as much to build for everyone so I raised $1 million. Now we've raised $142 million total. About 125 people, distributed team.

chapters.title: This Week in Fintech Acquired by Plaid
chapters.text:
Nick: We've been building This Week in Fintech for 6.5 years. Started as a fintech newsletter, became a fintech media company with over 200,000 subscribers.

Plaid approached us 5 months ago. I thought we'd talk about a partnership. They said they want to acquire the business to improve the conversation around fintech.

The best comp is Bloomberg with Bloomberg Business Week — complementary properties that work independently.

Fintech companies are actually taking market share from incumbents now. Ramp is a great example — grew too quickly, downsized, now crushing and shipping weekly.

Tech media hasn't caught up to how well some fintech companies are doing. We're looking to go much deeper on fintech coverage. Events and international are big parts of our DNA.

chapters.title: Zach Perret on Plaid's AI Integration and the Future of Fintech
chapters.text:
Zach Perret: We launched a Perplexity integration for the finance tab — stock portfolio in real time. This is the first step in consumer-facing AI experiences.

The core of Plaid doesn't change much — a pipe to make accounts, a data store, and analytics. But the entry point changes a lot. We're working on how to build a one-shot vibe coder get-started experience.

We saw roughly 200% increase in hobbyist developers building on platform in 2 months.

For coding, I want engineers using cutting edge everything. For products like transaction categorization, we don't want to upgrade models too quickly because customers build on top of our categories. For support, we're often using a model a turn or two back because cost is lower and quality is the same.

On agents and money: For as far as I can see, agents using credit cards will be the primary thing. Merchants accept credit cards, not stablecoins. I expect 5,000 YC companies launching agentic credit cards soon. Stablecoins at big banks are probably coming but not fast.

We're retooling the company — engineering pods went from 8 people to 4 people. Effects conference coming in a couple months with a bunch of AI launches.

chapters.title: Episode Wrap-up and Bob Iger's Secret Social Media Life
chapters.text:
Speaker 1: The Daily Beast reported that Bob Iger, the 75 year old Disney executive who stepped down as CEO, had a private Instagram account for friends and family with kayaking photos.

Speaker 2: John Palmer's wife's company Row 7 was ranked more innovative than TBPN in Fast Company. Row 7 makes seeds — a vegetable company dedicated to deliciousness. They're inventing vegetables. We reinvented business television.

Speaker 1: It ain't over till it's over. We're coming for you. It's personal now. We're not stopping till we get to number 41. Thank you for watching. Sign up for the newsletter at tbpn.com and we'll see you tomorrow at 11 AM Pacific.
